metadata.subject: English Language
metadata.description: Activity book for sixth grade preparatory students covering careers, conditional sentences, reported speech, and language skills

sections.title: Careers and Duties
sections.definition: Matching careers with their corresponding duties and responsibilities.
sections.explanation: Different careers have specific duties. Match each job with what the person in that job does.
sections.notes: Jobs include: architect, cameraman, engineer, flight attendant, journalist, lawyer, lifeguard, pilot, plumber, surgeon, secretary, tour guide

examples.problem: What does a pilot do?
examples.solution: A pilot flies a plane.

examples.problem: What does a surgeon do?
examples.solution: A surgeon operates on people in hospital.

sections.title: Word Web - Vocabulary Learning Strategy
sections.definition: A good way to learn new words is to make a word web.
sections.explanation: Choose a topic you like. Write it in the middle of a big piece of paper. Write words in English or in your language as they come into your head. Use a dictionary if necessary.
sections.notes: Example: Word web based on the word 'jobs' - add related words like: salary, office, uniform, qualifications, experience

sections.title: Lesson 2: First Conditional
sections.definition: The first conditional is used to talk about possible situations in the present or future and their results.
sections.explanation: We usually have the present simple in the if clause. In the main clause, we can use will/won't, present continuous with future reference, going to, can, might, should, or an imperative.
sections.rules: Structure: If + present simple, main clause (will/won't/can/might/should/imperative). Unless means 'if not'. Can use present continuous in if clause for prolonged actions.
sections.notes: If it's raining tomorrow, we won't go for a picnic. If you get here early, we can work together. I'll be home around four, unless there's an emergency.

examples.problem: If I (not pass) the high school exam this year, I (take) it again next year.
examples.solution: If I don't pass the high school exam this year, I'll take it again next year.

examples.problem: If anyone (need) advice about their application forms, I (be) here tomorrow to help.
examples.solution: If anyone needs advice about their application forms, I'll be here tomorrow to help.

sections.title: Lesson 3: Second Conditional
sections.definition: The second conditional is used to talk about unreal or imaginary situations in the present or future.
sections.explanation: Use past simple in the if clause and would + infinitive in the main clause.
sections.rules: Structure: If + past simple, would + infinitive. Can also use could or might in the main clause.
sections.notes: If I had lots of money, I would buy a racehorse.

examples.problem: I (not have) to spend so long on my homework if I (have) a computer of my own.
examples.solution: I wouldn't have to spend so long on my homework if I had a computer of my own.

examples.problem: Where (you choose) to live if you (can) live anywhere in the world?
examples.solution: Where would you choose to live if you could live anywhere in the world?

sections.title: Lesson 4: Reported Speech
sections.definition: Reported speech is used when the speaker is conveying a message from someone else.
sections.explanation: In reported statements and negative sentences, we often move the verb one tense to the past. In reported questions, we do not use the question form of the verb.
sections.rules: Statements: change tenses (present to past, past to past perfect). Questions: use if/whether for Yes/No questions, keep question word for Wh- questions but use statement word order. Time/place references change (here → there, tomorrow → the next day).
sections.notes: Reporting verbs: suggest, advise, promise, agree, refuse

examples.problem: Zaha said: 'It has been my dream since childhood.'
examples.solution: Zaha said that it had been her dream since childhood.

examples.problem: Interviewer asked: 'When did you decide to be an architect?'
examples.solution: The interviewer asked Zaha when she had decided to be an architect.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Regrets - I wish / If only
sections.definition: We use If only or I wish + past perfect to express regret about the past.
sections.explanation: Use past perfect to talk about things that happened in the past that you wish had been different.
sections.rules: Structure: I wish + past perfect. If only + past perfect. Meaning: I regret that this happened or didn't happen.
sections.notes: I wish I hadn't gone to bed so late. If only you had told me about your problem.

examples.problem: Unfortunately, I ate three bars of chocolate. That's why I felt sick.
examples.solution: If only I hadn't eaten three bars of chocolate.

examples.problem: Unfortunately, he fell over during the race. That's why he didn't win.
examples.solution: I wish he hadn't fallen over during the race.

sections.title: Lesson 6: Third Conditional
sections.definition: The third conditional is used to talk about unreal situations in the past - things that did not happen and their imagined results.
sections.explanation: Use past perfect in the if clause and would have + past participle in the main clause.
sections.rules: Structure: If + past perfect, would have + past participle. Can also use could have or might have.
sections.notes: If I had known about her problems, I would have lent her some money.

examples.problem: If we (leave) a little earlier, we wouldn't have missed the bus.
examples.solution: If we had left a little earlier, we wouldn't have missed the bus.

examples.problem: We (not eat) at that restaurant if we had known it was so expensive.
examples.solution: We wouldn't have eaten at that restaurant if we had known it was so expensive.

sections.title: Lesson 7: Accommodation Forms
sections.definition: Vocabulary for completing accommodation and application forms.
sections.explanation: Common terms: dietary requirements, medical condition, allergies, smoking permit, details.
sections.notes: Form fields: Title, First name, Family name, Date of birth, Gender, Special dietary requirements, Medical condition, Allergies, Smoking

examples.problem: What information do you give about special food needs?
examples.solution: Dietary requirements

sections.title: Lesson 7: Adjectives from Nouns
sections.definition: Forming adjectives from nouns by adding suffixes.
sections.explanation: Add suffixes like -ed, -ial, -able, -ive, -ous, -ful to nouns to form adjectives.
sections.rules: qualification → qualified, residence → residential, comfort → comfortable, experience → experienced, tailor → tailored, friend → friendly

examples.problem: All our teachers and other staff members are _____ (qualification).
examples.solution: qualified

examples.problem: All our staff members are really _____ (friend).
examples.solution: friendly

sections.title: Lesson 8: Writing an Email about Summer Course
sections.definition: Writing an email to a friend inviting them to join an English course abroad.
sections.explanation: Include: duration, location preferences, accommodation choices, costs, reasons for preferences.
sections.rules: Email structure: Greeting, reason for writing, details about course (duration, location, accommodation), personal preferences with reasons, invitation, closing.
sections.notes: Key phrases: 'I was wondering if you would like to join me', 'Personally, I would prefer', 'I am open to your suggestions', 'I definitely think it will be worth it'

sections.title: Lesson 9: Conference Interpreter - Careers Focus
sections.definition: Reading about the career of a conference interpreter, focusing on Samira's experience.
sections.explanation: Interpreters need to be well-informed, know technical terms, and convey meaning rather than literal translation.
sections.notes: Key terms: conference interpreter, diploma, postgraduate, stressful, technical terms, literal translation

examples.problem: Why do interpreters need to read about the subject beforehand?
examples.solution: So they know the technical terms of the field and can translate accurately.

examples.problem: How did Samira translate the phrase 'Akl il-inab habba habba'?
examples.solution: She conveyed the meaning rather than doing a literal translation.

sections.title: Revision: Conditional Sentences
sections.definition: Review of first, second, and third conditional structures.
sections.explanation: First conditional: real possibility. Second conditional: unreal present/future. Third conditional: unreal past.
sections.rules: First: if + present, will + infinitive. Second: if + past, would + infinitive. Third: if + past perfect, would have + past participle.

examples.problem: If people don't get / doesn't get enough rest, they feel / feels tired.
examples.solution: If people don't get enough rest, they feel tired.

examples.problem: If you had remembered to set your alarm, you (not wake) up so late.
examples.solution: If you had remembered to set your alarm, you wouldn't have woken up so late.

sections.title: Revision: Reported Speech
sections.definition: Review of converting direct speech to reported speech.
sections.explanation: Change tenses, pronouns, time references, and word order appropriately.
sections.rules: Statements: change tense back. Questions: use statement word order, use if/whether for yes/no questions.

examples.problem: My mother told me: 'I will buy you my birthday present the next day.'
examples.solution: My mother told me she would buy me my birthday present the next day.

examples.problem: The teacher asked me: 'What are you doing?'
examples.solution: The teacher asked me what I was doing.

sections.title: Revision: I wish / If only
sections.definition: Review of expressing regrets about the past.
sections.explanation: Use past perfect after I wish or If only to express regret about something that happened or didn't happen.
sections.rules: I wish + past perfect. If only + past perfect.

examples.problem: I forgot Muna's birthday, and now she's mad at me.
examples.solution: I wish I hadn't forgotten Muna's birthday because now she's mad at me.

examples.problem: I didn't save enough money to buy the sneakers I want.
examples.solution: If only I had saved enough money to buy the sneakers I want.

sections.title: Vocabulary Revision
sections.definition: Review of careers and related vocabulary.
sections.explanation: Complete sentences with correct job titles and vocabulary words.
sections.notes: Key vocabulary: secretary, pilot, lifeguard, architect, tour guide, journalist, technology, research, highly qualified, needs, canteen, application, limit, interpreter

examples.problem: Salwa works with the director. She writes letters and answers the telephone. She is his _____
examples.solution: secretary

examples.problem: Captain Yousouf flies planes from Europe to the Gulf. He is a _____
examples.solution: pilot

sections.title: Writing Assignment: Advantages of Studying English in Britain
sections.definition: Write 100-120 words on the advantages of studying English in Britain.
sections.explanation: Consider: hearing English all day, opportunities to practise speaking, staying with British family, watching TV and films in English, reading newspapers, observing British way of life.
sections.rules: Essay should be 100-120 words. Include multiple advantages of immersion learning.
sections.notes: Key points: immersion in English environment, authentic language exposure, cultural experience, conversation practice with native speakers
